title: Joseph Reuben Bridges: A Lifetime on the Aroostook Potato Frontier
narrative: Joseph Reuben Bridges was born on 9 November 1837 in Bridgewater, in the far northeastern reaches of Maine's Aroostook County, the son of John Holland Bridges and Sarah (Barker / Thorncroft). His birth year places him among the very first generation of children raised in the borderland townships strung along the Maine-New Brunswick line. The township that would become Bridgewater had been laid out under Massachusetts before Maine's 1820 statehood and was carved from the original survey grants meant to fund public academies, but it was still raw country in 1837 and would not be formally incorporated as a town until 2 March 1858. To be born here in the late 1830s was to be born on a working frontier of cleared lots, stump fields, and dense forest, only a short distance from the Saint John River and the New Brunswick settlements beyond it.

His family's roots ran straight across that international boundary. Joseph's father, John Holland Bridges, was a New Brunswick-born settler (the family traces to Sheffield, NB) who, like many in the region, moved freely between the two sides of a border whose exact line was itself unsettled in those years. Surviving family genealogy records the marriage of a John Bridges to Sarah F. Thorncroft on 22 January 1837 at Houlton, Maine, the shire town of Aroostook, with Sarah said to have been born about 1818 in New Brunswick; this fits the borderland pattern of the family closely, though the precise reconciliation of the names Sarah Barker and Sarah F. Thorncroft is not something the free sources allow me to settle with confidence, and I note it honestly as an open question. The same family tradition remembers the Bridges men clearing the country's early roads, including an account that a Reuben Bridges helped his father grub out the Fort Road in 1856 and that a Holland Bridges kept a tavern in nearby Westfield, vivid glimpses of the labor of opening Aroostook even if such anecdotes carry the usual caution owed to compiled family records.

Joseph came of age in the shadow of hardship. His father John Holland Bridges died young, in 1857, when Joseph was still a young man of nineteen or twenty, leaving him to make his way in a region that demanded everything of those who stayed. The Aroostook of his youth was the country of the recently concluded Aroostook War: the bloodless 1838-39 confrontation over the Maine-New Brunswick boundary had been settled, the line surveyed, and iron boundary monuments set in the early 1840s, finally giving legal definition to the borderland in which his family lived and farmed. The early settlers of these townships, drawn from New England and from across the border in Canada and Ireland, had already discovered that Aroostook's well-drained loam and cool northern climate were extraordinarily suited to the potato, and the crop that would define the County for a century was taking hold in exactly the years Joseph grew to manhood.

At some point Joseph married Hannah Joan Wood, born 11 April 1847, whose family was rooted at McAdam, New Brunswick, and who by 1870 was connected to the Wood household at Mars Hill (her father, John R. Wood, appears there as a farmer). The marriage joined two borderland families in the classic Aroostook manner, a Maine-born man of New Brunswick ancestry wedding a New Brunswick-born woman whose people had likewise settled on the Maine side. The couple made their home and raised their family at Mars Hill, the township at the base of Mars Hill Mountain that had grown up around timber-cutting and milling and was incorporated as a town on 21 February 1867. There they spent the long middle and later decades of their lives as the surrounding forest gave way, decade by decade, to the open potato fields for which the region became famous.

Joseph's working life spanned the transformation of Aroostook from isolated frontier to Maine's great agricultural region. For much of his early farming years the County remained cut off from distant markets, its potatoes hard to move and its economy a mix of subsistence farming and lumbering. That changed decisively with the railroad: the Bangor and Aroostook reached Mars Hill in 1892, and the ability to ship the crop to East Coast markets turned Aroostook's soil and climate into a genuine "Potato Empire." A Mars Hill farmer of Joseph's generation thus lived through the precise moment when the back-breaking, locally bounded agriculture of his youth became part of a national commerce, even as the fundamental rhythms of a northern farm, the long winters, the spring planting, and the great autumn potato harvest, endured unchanged.

Among Joseph and Hannah's children was a daughter, Gertrude Evelyn Bridges, born 16 March 1873 at Mars Hill, who in time married Otis B. Danforth and eventually moved south to Troy in Waldo County, carrying the family's line out of Aroostook and into the central Maine countryside; it is through Gertrude that this branch connects to the present family. The broad documentary footing for the household is reasonably good for the place and period: Maine's vital records of the era and Mars Hill's own town records cover these years, and Joseph, Hannah, and other kin are commemorated in Pierce Cemetery at Mars Hill, the burying ground that anchors the family to the town.

Joseph Reuben Bridges died on 14 August 1913 at Mars Hill, Aroostook County, at the age of seventy-five, and was buried in Pierce Cemetery there; his widow Hannah outlived him by two decades, dying on 31 October 1933 and resting in the same cemetery. His life, from his 1837 birth on the Bridgewater frontier to his death in 1913, traced almost exactly the arc of nineteenth-century Aroostook: born when the boundary was still in dispute and the woods scarcely broken, he lived to see the railroad arrive, the potato fields spread across the County, and the borderland of his parents' generation settle into the established farming country of northern Maine.
